summary: This post continues a series on migrating from Oracle to Microsoft SQL Server, focusing specifically on performance challenges with recursive queries. The author encountered significant issues translating Oracle's optimized SYS_CONNECT_BY_PATH function to SQL Server CTEs, especially in complex nested views. The solution involved using inline user-defined functions and temp tables to dramatically improve query response times from non-responsive to 1-5 seconds. The approach maintained transparency for end users and applications by wrapping functions in views.
key_points: Oracle's SYS_CONNECT_BY_PATH is highly optimized and has no direct SQL Server equivalent, causing major performance issues during migration
CTE views work for simple recursive queries but fail with complex nested scenarios
Inline user-defined functions with temp tables can solve recursive query performance issues
Storing complex queries in temp tables before recursive operations simplifies the optimizer's work
The solution maintains user transparency by wrapping functions in views
money_quotes: This function is well know by the Oracle optimizer which makes this function unbelievable fast and there is no similar function on SQL Server
I had good performance measures on an easy simple query but invinitive response with more complex
In my case it solve the performance issue from not responding to 1-5 second each view
I stored this complex query to a temp table and queried recursively on this temp-table. With this the complexity for the recursive query disappeared
key_insight: When migrating Oracle recursive queries to SQL Server, inline user-defined functions with temp tables can overcome the performance limitations of CTEs for complex nested views.
